topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt harsh, exposing every flaw in the canvas. Elias traced the line of the figure, a swirling vortex of despair rendered in charcoal and ink. Then he saw it—a tiny, almost invisible detail on the lower arm of the subject, a delicate, precise script. It wasn't part of his composition. It was a tattoo. A signature, perhaps, or a secret mark. A cold knot tightened in his chest, a familiar, sickening lurch. He remembered the frantic energy of that night, the hurried exchange, the way his own hand had felt clumsy and desperate as he handed over the final piece. Now, looking at that mark, a profound sense of having misjudged the transaction washed over him. He wanted to scrub the paint off the wall, to erase the entire endeavor, but his fingers remained frozen above the brush.

[story 2]

Clara stood before the finished oil painting, the vibrant blues and deep violets seeming to mock her. It was stunning, technically flawless, but beneath the surface, a tremor ran through her hands. Hidden beneath the shadow of a particularly expressive eye was a small, intricate pattern—a tattoo. It was someone else’s mark, undeniably placed there by a hand that was not hers. A wave of nausea rose, making her grip the edge of the easel so tightly her knuckles bleached white. She replayed the encounter in her mind: the flattery, the misplaced trust, the way she’d allowed herself to be so open. A heavy sigh escaped her lips, a sound thin and brittle in the quiet studio. She slowly lowered her gaze, the beauty of the art suddenly tainted by a profound sense of trespass.

[story 3]

Marcus leaned in close, examining the texture of the bronze sculpture. His breath hitched when his eyes snagged on the back of the drapery—a minuscule, dark etching that wasn't sculpted detail but applied, permanent. A jolt of stark realization shot through him. It was someone else’s work, someone else’s indelible mark embedded within his creation. He felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to flee, to dismantle the entire installation. He remembered the conversation, the hurried exchange of payment, the fleeting moment where his own ambition had eclipsed any sense of respect for the source material. He turned away sharply, the cool metal of the sculpture suddenly feeling heavy and accusatory in his grasp.
